title: About
text: About this blog

title: Welcome To A Safe Space For Understanding
text:
“I Swear, You’re Not Stupid” is a sanctuary for the intellectually curious and the perpetually befuddled alike. Nestled within my portfolio, this blog is a vibrant testament to the journey of continual learning in the ever-evolving realms of data science, analytics, and visualization.


In the world of data, the leap from academic theory to professional practice can often feel like venturing into a chasm vast and unknown. Despite degrees lining our walls and accolades adorning our resumes, the moment arrives when we stare at a dataset or a regression analysis and wonder, “Have I ever truly understood this?”



via GIPHY


“I Swear, You’re Not Stupid” is a compilation of insights, learnings, and revisitations of concepts we thought we knew but find anew in different lights. Here, you’ll find an array of posts dedicated to unraveling the complexities of data science and analytics – from the foundational stones of statistical analysis in R and Python to the intricate art of data visualization.


This blog isn’t just a repository; it’s a dialogue between us. It’s for the seasoned professional who momentarily forgot the formula for calculating the standard error, for the recent graduate who’s never applied regression outside a classroom setting, and for the curious wanderer who believes that learning is an endless journey.


In this small space of the internet, I’d hope readers will be reminded that understanding comes not from the ability to recite, but the capacity to apply. Hopefully, through one post at a time, I can support you from knowing to understanding, from theory to practice, from doubt to confidence.


Together, we’ll delve into the principles that drive our field, armed with the conviction that, indeed, you’re not stupid – sometimes, we just need to see things a little differently.

title: All About Regression
text:
There’s tons to learn about regression, but I’ll just give you a quick overview. 
Types of Regression

There are several types of regression analysis, including:



Linear Regression: Predicts a dependent variable using a linear equation with one or more independent variables. It’s the most straightforward type of regression, used when the relationship between variables is linear.
Logistic Regression: Used for binary outcomes (e.g., success/failure). It estimates probabilities using a logistic function, making it suitable for classification problems.
Polynomial Regression: Extends linear regression by allowing the model to fit nonlinear relationships through polynomial terms of independent variables.
Ridge and Lasso Regression: These are extensions of linear regression that include regularization terms to prevent overfitting, with Lasso also allowing for feature selection by shrinking some coefficients to zero.



Note: For more information, check out this article.


Linear Regression
Here’s what linear regression looks like:


Logistic Regression
logistic regression is a type of regression that is used to predict a binary outcome.

title: Insight through Data
text:
All data in this case study is anonymized to protect confidentiality. Details have been altered, but the depicted processes and insights are accurately represented.



In my role as the Data Insights Specialist II on the Learning and Impact team at the Center for Employment Opportunities, I directed the overall strategy for each quarterly board report. I identified key performance indicators (KPIs) by analyzing data and aligning them with the strategic goals of the organization. I coordinated the narrative, deciding which insights to present, and shaped the story to highlight our achievements and areas for improvement. I’d communicate these insights through visualizations that were clear and impactful.
Here, I’ll share a glimpse into how I’d transform raw data [from various departments] into engaging visual stories that inform strategic and operational decisions. It’s all about turning complex data into clear, useful insights, even for those who aren’t familiar with data science or statistical jargon. 
Each quarter, I set these goals for myself:

Show Org-Wide Performance: Ensure strategic alignment with fiscal year objectives.
Share Our Strategy for Improvement: Highlight key initiatives and outcomes
Delivering Actionable Insights: Provide clear, impactful information to the board.

In my role as the Data Insights Specialist II on the Learning and Impact team at the Center for Employment Opportunities, I directed the overall strategy for each quarterly board report. It’s all about turning complex data into clear, useful insights, even for those who aren’t familiar with data science or statistical jargon. 
Each quarter, I set these goals for myself:

Show Org-Wide Performance: Ensure strategic alignment with fiscal year objectives.
Share Our Strategy for Improvement: Highlight key initiatives and outcomes
Delivering Actionable Insights: Provide clear, impactful information to the board.

title: Insight through Data
text:
Wrangling the Data
At the end of each quarter, I’d reach out to various departments to gather updates on programs and activities. As the Data Insights Specialist II, I stayed informed on organizational activities year-round, as our team was heavily involved in evaluations and research. Proactivley engaging with pilots, program performance, evaluations, research, and analyses allowed me to know exactly what to ask for when compiling the board report, ensuring relevant insights were included.
The Data
Usually, these updates came back to me as rudimentary figures, spreadsheets, or dashboards from which I’d pull data points. I would then download these into .csv files and process them using my favorite tool: pandas.





Via G-chat: Quick messages containing key data points or updates.







Through Google Sheets: More structured data in spreadsheets shared across departments.







Inside Emails: Often, data came embedded within the text of an email, requiring careful extraction and verification.




In addition to incorporating relevant program updates, we also included findings from the Learning and Impact team’s internal analyses. One quarter, we included insights derived from an evaluation of one of our intervention methods. These insights revealed specific characteristics and intervention dosages that significantly increased the likelihood of success for our participants.
“Likelihood”—there’s a term you’ll often hear in data science. Within the Learning and Impact team and in discussions across departments, terms like probabilities and likelihoods were straightforward to discuss, thanks to the context we could provide. However, translating these and other statistical terms accurately into language and visuals for the board—without the benefit of interactive dialogue—posed a unique challenge.
In the code blocks that follow, which I’ve anonymized for confidentiality, I’ll demonstrate how I transformed the insights from our intervention analysis into visuals for our board report. The data cleaning, processing, and manipulation steps shown below are routine practices I used to prepare the data points received from various departments. These steps were crucial for converting raw data, whether received through G-chat, Google Sheets, or emails, into clear, impactful board-level visual presentations.

# Setting Up Environment
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Read in Data
df = pd.read_csv("q3_program_data.csv")


df.head()


df.dtypes


df.columns = df.columns.str.lower()
# df[[date]] = pd.to_datetime(df[date])

title: Insight through Data
text:
Designing Clear Visuals
After spending time with the data through—cleaning, pivoting, and generating initial visuals like bar charts—I begin to see potential ways to make the data understandable for the board. One best practice I adhere to in my visualization work is the “30-second rule” (sometimes it’s more like 2 minutes). The idea is that viewers should can quickly grasp the intent of a chart or graph. This principle contrasts with more abstract data visualizations, which, while beautiful and engaging, cater to a different audience. Artists like Federica Fragipane and Alberto Cairo, who I admire greatly, often take artistic liberties that suit their intended audiences.
For board presentations, I focus on straightforward designs, avoiding overly complex or abstract visuals. Sketching with pencil and paper after extensive screen time helps me connect physically with my work, making the creative process more tangible and refreshing.
